design_decisions.address_space_new: Symmetric copy-the-live-root on both arches (NO TTBR1/TTBR0 split). arch::address_space_new() frame_alloc()s one 4 KiB top-level table and memcpy's ALL 512 entries of the live root into it: x86_64 reads CR3&PTE_ADDR_MASK -> *const u64 and write_volatile/read_volatile-copies 512 words into the new PML4; aarch64 reads TTBR0_EL1&TTBR_BADDR_MASK, copies 512 words into the new L1, then dsb_ishst to publish. The new frame is identity-mapped low RAM / RAM gigabyte (PA==VA) so it is written directly. This shares EVERY kernel mapping by reference (x86_64 identity PML4[0], M4 user [1], M7 heap [2], M8 LAPIC [3]; aarch64 Device L1[0], RAM L1[1], M3 L1[2], M4 L1[3], M7 heap L1[4]). lib.rs wraps the returned root PA in an opaque Copy AddressSpace{root:u64} and, on the FIRST create, BOOT_ROOT.compare_exchange(0, arch::current_root()) captures the boot root (the live root then IS the boot root, since no switch has happened). Returns None on M6 OOM (fail-closed).
design_decisions.map_in_root: New tb-hal primitive arch::map_in_root(root_pa, va, pa, writable) -> bool walks/creates tables from the EXPLICIT root_pa (NOT the live root) reusing the existing unsafe ensure_table/level_index/make_entry + M6 frame_alloc for intermediates. x86_64: PML4->PDPT->PD->PT, leaf = PTE_P|PTE_NX (+PTE_RW if writable), no invlpg (root not live; 0->valid never cached). aarch64: a helper `unsafe fn map_one_in_root` walks L1->L2->L3, leaf = PAGE_KERNEL_RW_NX (+ AP[2]=1<<7 when !writable for EL1 read-only), then map_in_root publishes with dsb_ishst;isb. Because each entity root is a COPY, writing a top-level entry in a vacant slot affects only that root. lib.rs exposes map_in_space(space, va, pa, writable) -> arch::map_in_root(space.root, ...). Returns false on M6 OOM with no half-mapped page.
design_decisions.as_switch_foldin: Folds into lib.rs yield_to() BEFORE arch::ctx_switch: a new private fn switch_address_space(prev, next) is called right after CURRENT_TASK.store(next.0) and before the unsafe ctx_switch. It reads a parallel static TASK_AS:[AtomicU64;MAX_TASKS] (0 = sentinel 'default boot space'); if TASK_AS[prev]==TASK_AS[next] it returns with NO control-register touch (the M2/M9 case where every task is root 0 -> byte-for-byte unchanged, incl. aarch64 MMU-off M2), else it resolves the sentinel 0 to the captured BOOT_ROOT and calls arch::switch_root(target) (a `target==0` guard makes a CR3/TTBR0=0 write impossible). x86_64 switch_root = write_cr3(root&PTE_ADDR_MASK) (flushes non-global TLB; shared kernel-half identity keeps stack/code/serial valid). aarch64 switch_root = msr ttbr0_el1; isb; tlbi vmalle1is; dsb ish; isb (no ASID -> global EL1&0 flush). task_set_address_space(task, space) tags a task's TASK_AS slot. The swap happens before ctx_switch so the next task's stack/regs are read under its own translation; since every root is a full copy of the boot root, the yield_to code + both stacks stay mapped across the flip.
design_decisions.test_va_slots: x86_64: PML4[4] = 0x0000_0200_0000_0000 (kernel uses PML4[0..3]: identity, M4 user, M7 heap, M8 LAPIC). aarch64: L1[6] = 0x0000_0001_8000_0000 (6 GiB; kernel uses L1[0..4]: Device, RAM, M3, M4, M7 heap; L1[5]/[6] vacant; within the 39-bit T0SZ=25 VA, canonical). The kernel declares M10_TEST_VA per-arch via #[cfg(target_arch=...)]. Because address_space_new copies the boot root (which leaves these top-level slots empty), map_in_root builds the slot's sub-tree ONLY inside each entity root, so a private write there never perturbs the boot root or another entity.
design_decisions.cross_space_fault_test: Provoked from the boot task under the DEFAULT root, where the test VA is VACANT (the slot is only ever filled in the entity roots). The kernel arms statics (M10_FAULT_ARMED, M10_FAULT_VA=test VA, M10_FAULT_HOME_ROOT=space_e.root_pa(), M10_FAULT_SEEN=false) then calls tb_hal::addr_load(test_va) -> #PF (x86 vec14, fault_addr=CR2) / data abort (aarch64 EC 0x24/0x25, fault_addr=FAR_EL1) -> TrapKind::PageFault -> dispatch_trap -> the SAFE kernel trap_hook. The hook's guarded arm `TrapKind::PageFault if M10_FAULT_ARMED && (info.fault_addr & !0xFFF)==(M10_FAULT_VA & !0xFFF)` (page-aligned compare, per review-1 fix) sets SEEN=true, DISARMS (so a later stray fault still halts), GUARDED-RESUMES by calling tb_hal::address_space_switch_root(home root) -> flips the LIVE root to space E (where the VA IS mapped) -> returns TrapAction::Resume. Both arches re-execute the faulting instruction on Resume (x86 #PF saved-RIP is AT the faulting insn and __alltraps balances the error code with `add rsp,16` before iretq; aarch64 advances ELR ONLY for brk, leaving data aborts at the faulting insn), so addr_load returns MAGIC_E. NO allocation in the fault handler (the safer path; gen:correctness's in-#PF frame_alloc is NOT grafted). The kernel asserts SEEN && recovered==MAGIC_E, then address_space_switch_default()s. Runs single-threaded with the timer disarmed.
design_decisions.kernel_half_sharing: address_space_new memcpy's the FULL 512-entry top-level table of the live kernel root into each entity root, so the kernel half is byte-identical (shared BY REFERENCE) in every root: x86_64 identity [0,1GiB) (code/stack) in PML4[0], M7 heap PML4[2], M8 LAPIC PML4[3]; aarch64 RAM gigabyte (code/stack) in L1[1] and the PL011 UART Device gigabyte in L1[0]. So after a CR3/TTBR0 flip the kernel keeps fetching code, using its stack, and writing serial (x86 COM1 port-I/O is CR3-independent; aarch64 PL011 MMIO lives in the shared L1[0]) -- including inside the trap hook that itself flips the root during recovery. The self-test prints over serial after each switch to prove it, and re-asserts M3 under entity roots. CAVEAT (documented in code + ROADMAP §3): a NEW top-level KERNEL mapping created AFTER an AddressSpace exists would not propagate into that copy; none happens during M10 (the only post-creation top-level write is the private test VA into entity roots only). M12+ must pre-reserve kernel top-level slots or fan late entries out / move to the TTBR1 split. NO serial.rs / TCR / EPD1 / TTBR1 / TSS.rsp0 change.
design_decisions.m3_reassert: M10 does NOT touch mmu_init/mmu_selftest, serial.rs, TCR/EPD1, or add a TTBR1 split, so the original M3 block still prints 'M3: mmu OK' unchanged earlier in the same boot (mmu_selftest is single-shot with a re-run guard and is NOT re-called). M10 ACTIVELY re-asserts M3 via a new tb-hal facade m3_test_va_intact() that reads the M3 self-test VA (x86 0x4000_0000 under PML4[0], expecting module-level MAGIC_B; aarch64 0x8000_0000 under L1[2], expecting a local M3_MAGIC_B=0xB22B_B22B_CAFE_F00D mirroring mmu_selftest's fn-local const) and compares to the post-remap magic. The kernel calls it under the DEFAULT root AND under entity space_e (then switches back): both read true because the M3 sub-tree hangs off a top-level slot copied by reference into every space -- proving the M3 kernel-half mapping survives every address-space switch. The textbook TTBR1(kernel)/TTBR0(entity) split + ASIDs + x86 PCID / per-task TSS.rsp0 are documented as deferred M11/M12 refinements.

pub use mmu::{heap_window, map_heap_frames, mmu_init, mmu_selftest};

# scripts/run-x86_64.sh - QEMU launcher + Definition-of-Done check for M9 (x86_64).
#
# Boots the PVH ELF kernel under QEMU 'microvm' (the machine type Firecracker is
# modelled on), wires legacy 16550 COM1 to stdio, and asserts the EXACT marker
# "M9: preempt OK" appears on serial (the newest cumulative-boot milestone; on a
# timer tick the kernel involuntarily round-robin switches kernel tasks from
# interrupt context). M0's hello, M1's trap round-trip, M2's ping-pong,
# "M3: mmu OK", "M4: user/ring OK", "M5: alloc OK", "M6: frame alloc OK",
# "M7: heap OK" and "M8: timer OK" all print earlier in the same boot, so one run
# proves every milestone. Fail-closed:
patches.new_string:
# scripts/run-x86_64.sh - QEMU launcher + Definition-of-Done check for M10 (x86_64).
#
# Boots the PVH ELF kernel under QEMU 'microvm' (the machine type Firecracker is
# modelled on), wires legacy 16550 COM1 to stdio, and asserts the EXACT marker
# "M10: addrspace OK" appears on serial (the newest cumulative-boot milestone;
# each schedulable entity runs in its own top-level page table -- one cannot
# read/write another's private memory -- while the kernel half stays mapped, and
# the address-space switch folds into the M9 context switch). M0's hello, M1's
# trap round-trip, M2's ping-pong, "M3: mmu OK", "M4: user/ring OK",
# "M5: alloc OK", "M6: frame alloc OK", "M7: heap OK", "M8: timer OK" and
# "M9: preempt OK" all print earlier in the same boot, so one run proves every
# milestone. Fail-closed:

patches.old_string: MARKER='M9: preempt OK'
patches.new_string: MARKER='M10: addrspace OK'

patches.old_string:
### M10 — Per-agent address spaces (memory isolation) · `M10: addrspace OK`
Each schedulable entity runs in its own top-level page table — one cannot read or
write another's memory — while the kernel half stays mapped across every switch.
An `AddressSpace` object = a fresh top-level table (M6 frames, M3 typed layer, M7
heap-tracked). x86_64: per-entity PML4 sharing the kernel higher-half; switch =
`mov CR3` + update `TSS.rsp0` (PCID deferred). aarch64: kernel to `TTBR1_EL1`,
each entity its own `TTBR0_EL1`; switch = write `TTBR0` + ASID + `tlbi`/`isb`.
The swap folds into the M9 switch. *Risk:* aarch64 **requires refactoring M3's
identity-only TTBR0 into a TTBR1/TTBR0 split** — touches an already-green
milestone, so the DoD must **re-assert M3**. Self-test: two tasks in two address
spaces map the same VA to different private frames, each writes/reads its own
magic, a switch flips the root, kernel/serial survive, a cross-space access faults.
patches.new_string:
### M10 — Per-agent address spaces (memory isolation) · `M10: addrspace OK`
Each schedulable entity runs in its own top-level page table — one cannot read or
write another's memory — while the kernel half stays mapped across every switch.
An `AddressSpace` object = a fresh top-level table (one M6 frame, M3 typed layer)
that COPIES the entire live kernel root into itself, so every existing kernel
mapping (identity RAM, serial, the M7 heap window, the M8 device window, the M3
test mapping) is shared by reference and the kernel half is identical in every
entity. Private pages (`map_in_space` → the new `tb-hal` `map_in_root` primitive)
land in a top-level slot the kernel root leaves vacant (x86_64 `PML4[4]`, aarch64
`L1[6]`), so they are visible only through that root. **Both arches are
symmetric** (no TTBR1/TTBR0 split): switch = `mov CR3` (x86_64, flushes
non-global TLB) / `msr TTBR0_EL1` + `isb; tlbi vmalle1is; dsb ish; isb` (aarch64,
no ASID yet). The swap folds into the M9 `yield_to` (a parallel `TASK_AS[]` of
per-task roots; it flips only when the next task's root differs). The textbook
`TTBR1`(kernel)/`TTBR0`(entity) split + ASIDs + PCID are a **deferred
refinement** (M11/M12); M10 ships the lower-risk copy-the-root design and so does
NOT touch `mmu_init` — `mmu_selftest` + `M3: mmu OK` print unchanged, and M10
actively **re-asserts M3** by reading the M3 test VA under each entity root.
Self-test: two tasks in two address spaces map the same VA to different private
frames, each writes/reads its own magic and sees only its own, a switch flips the
root, kernel/serial survive, and a cross-space access faults (observed by the
trap hook + a guarded resume).

patches.new_string:
5. **Address spaces (M10) kept separate** from the agent milestone because it
   touches the already-green M3 paging — a regression risk that earns its own
   marker and an M3 re-assertion. (As shipped, M10 is **symmetric** across both
   arches via the copy-the-live-root design — no TTBR1/TTBR0 split — leaving
   `mmu_init` untouched; the textbook split + ASIDs are a deferred M11/M12 refinement.)

patches.old_string:
- **M10 on aarch64** touches the already-green M3 (TTBR split) → the DoD re-asserts
  M3. x86_64 stays a single CR3 swap.
patches.new_string:
- **M10 on aarch64** touches the already-green M3 → the DoD re-asserts M3. As
  shipped it AVOIDS the TTBR1/TTBR0 split: both arches copy the live kernel root
  into each entity table (kernel half shared by reference) and swap a single root
  register (CR3 / TTBR0_EL1 + `tlbi vmalle1is`); `mmu_init` is untouched. The
  textbook TTBR1(kernel)/TTBR0(entity) split + ASIDs are a deferred M11/M12 refinement.

patches.new_string:

| **M11** (capability handle table + object model + syscall ABI) | ⏳ **in progress** (next increment) |
| M12 – M18 | ⬜ planned (this document) |

selftest: Build is the arbiter (orchestrator applies this edit-set to the CLEAN tree at dd20396; nothing was written to disk). (1) `cargo kbuild --target targets/x86_64-tabos-none.json` AND `targets/aarch64-tabos-none.json` must compile 0 warnings / 0 errors. kernel/ stays unsafe-free: the M10 block only calls the safe tb_hal facade + STACK_G/H.take() + Task::from_raw; ALL new unsafe/asm is in crates/tb-hal (arch/*/mmu.rs CR3/TTBR0 reads+writes, table copy/walk via the existing ensure_table; lib.rs addr_load/addr_store_load raw VA derefs). (2) Boot x86_64 QEMU microvm (scripts/run-x86_64.sh): expect the cumulative stream M0 hello, M1/M2 markers, 'M3: mmu OK', 'M4: user/ring OK', 'M5: alloc OK', 'M6: frame alloc OK', 'M7: heap OK', 'M8: timer OK', 'M9: preempt OK', then the new M10 lines 'addrspace-test: building two private address spaces', 'addrspace: task G wrote+read its magic under space E', '... task H ... space F', 'addrspace: both tasks saw only their own magic', 'addrspace: same VA -> different private frame per root', 'addrspace: cross-space access faulted and recovered', and finally 'M10: addrspace OK' (the script greps this exact marker), then halt. (3) aarch64 QEMU virt (scripts/run-aarch64.sh): same stream + 'M10: addrspace OK'. (4) tb-vmm (scripts/run-vmm-x86_64.sh, if /dev/kvm): same. Manual review confirmed against the live files: (a) kernel/src/main.rs has NO unsafe{} blocks (only #[unsafe(no_mangle)] on rust_main + #[panic_handler]); (b) trap_hook's new arm fires only on TrapKind::PageFault AND M10_FAULT_ARMED with a page-aligned (& !0xFFF) compare, so M1 #BP (kind-check short-circuit) and M8/M9 IRQs (timer::try_handle_irq returns before classification on x86; aarch64 IRQs use a different vector) are byte-for-byte unaffected, and genuine faults still hit the fatal `_` arm; (c) yield_to's switch_address_space is a no-op for every root-0 M2/M9 task (prev_as==next_as==0 -> early return, no CR3/TTBR0 write), so M2 ping-pong and M9 preemption are unchanged; (d) both arch trap handlers classify the probe as TrapKind::PageFault with fault_addr=CR2/FAR and re-execute the faulting instruction on Resume (x86 __alltraps balances the #PF error code with `add rsp,16` before iretq; aarch64 advances ELR only for brk), so recovered==MAGIC_E holds; (e) 'M3: mmu OK' still prints from its untouched M3 block, and is additionally re-asserted under the default + entity roots via m3_test_va_intact(); (f) `grep -rn 'M9: preempt OK' scripts` now appears only in earlier-marker prose; the MARKER/FINAL_MARKER vars and headers read M10.
build_notes: Compile gotchas verified against the live sources at dd20396: (1) x86_64 write_cr3 is `#[allow(dead_code)]` + `unsafe fn`; switch_root now uses it, so it is no longer dead — the #[allow] stays and is harmless (Rust never lints a now-redundant #[allow]). No new warning. (2) aarch64 has NO module-level MAGIC_B: the M3 magic 0xB22B_B22B_CAFE_F00D is a fn-local const inside mmu_selftest, so aarch64 m3_test_va_intact redeclares its own `const M3_MAGIC_B` (value mirrored); x86_64 reuses the module-level TEST_VA (usize) + MAGIC_B (u64) consts already present. (3) tb-hal lib.rs has `#![deny(missing_docs)]` — every new pub item (AddressSpace, root_pa, address_space_new, map_in_space, address_space_switch[_default/_root], task_set_address_space, addr_store_load, addr_load, m3_test_va_intact) carries a doc comment; switch_address_space/TASK_AS/BOOT_ROOT are private but documented. (4) The new arch fns are all reachable (lib.rs uses each via arch::*), so no dead_code on the library surface; existing unsafe fns (zero_table/ensure_table/map_one_heap_page) call unsafe ops without inner `unsafe{}`, proving the crate does NOT deny unsafe_op_in_unsafe_fn — the new aarch64 `unsafe fn map_one_in_root` follows that same pattern, no warning. (5) kernel main.rs imports already cover everything: AtomicBool/AtomicU64/AtomicUsize/Ordering (line 59) and Task/TaskStack/TrapAction/TrapInfo/TrapKind (line 60); M10_TEST_VA is cfg-gated per arch (exactly one compiled per target) and is used, so no unused-const. (6) The trap_hook addition is a guarded match arm on info.kind whose guard reads info.fault_addr — legal; ordered before the `_` arm so it stays exhaustive with no unreachable-pattern warning. (7) aarch64 switch_root uses inline asm WITHOUT `nomem` (a full address-space switch changes how memory translates) and WITHOUT an ASID, so it `tlbi vmalle1is` on every switch (correct, slightly heavier; ASIDs are the deferred refinement); options(nostack, preserves_flags) are valid (no stack use, NZCV untouched). (8) The route-boot->G-through-TASK_G_RAW change keeps the local `task_g` live (still used by TASK_G_RAW.store + task_set_address_space), so no unused-variable warning, while removing the dead-weight static flagged by review 3. (9) m10_fail is a diverging closure (`-> !`) used in match None arms — coerces to any type, mirroring the existing M9 m9_fail closure, so it compiles. (10) ROADMAP-V2.md patches embed Unicode copied byte-for-byte from the live file (em-dash —, middot ·, arrow →, en-dash – in 'M12 – M18', ✅/⏳/⬜); they are doc-only and do not gate the build. Open verification gap (could not be closed without writing to disk, which the rules forbid): the two `cargo kbuild` targets + three QEMU/tb-vmm boots were reasoned through statically rather than executed; every anchor old_string was confirmed byte-exact via Read against the clean tree, and the resume-from-#PF / data-abort paths were validated against the actual trap.rs handlers.
